coredata.dc:title: Luminescence and photosensitization performances of carbon dots for ratiometric fluorescent sensing of glyphosate
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2023 The Authors. Published by Elsevier Ltd on behalf of Changchun Institute of Applied Chemistry Chinese Academy of Sciences.

ZHENG, Yu
LI, Xinyi
BA, Lulu
WEI, Caixia
GAO, Yawen
ZHANG, Kui
coredata.dc:description: Exploring the practical applications of luminescent carbon dots (CDs) plays an important role in analytical chemistry. In this study, maleic anhydride and polyethyleneimine derived phosphorescent CDs can generate oxidative singlet oxygen and superoxide radical under UV light irradiation. The o-phenylenediamine (OPD) was used as a chromogenic agent, which can be oxidized by singlet oxygen and superoxide radical to produce intense yellow color fluorescence. Furthermore, the CDs-photosensitized oxidation of OPD was inhibited by Cu2+ ions. Because the high affinity between glyphosate and Cu2+, CDs-photosensitized oxidation can take place when glyphosate is added into the Cu2+-mediated system. Utilizing the blue color fluorescence of CDs as internal standard, a ratiometric fluorescent method for glyphosate detection was established. The proposed method can be performed on a smartphone, which exhibits a satisfactory performance for detection of glyphosate residue in real samples rapidly, conveniently, and effectively.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Luminescence
Photosensitization
Ratiometric sensing
Glyphosate

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Oxidation of OPD using CDs as photosensitizer
Detection of glyphosate by photosensitization-mediated oxidation
Light-driven oxidation of OPD using CDs

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Schematic representation of the preparation procedure for the CDs; (b) TEM image of synthesized CDs; (c) particle size distributions of the CDs; (d) 3D fluorescence spectra of CDs dispersed in water; (e) 3D phosphorescence spectra of CDs in PVA composite film.

(a) Time-dependent UV-vis absorption spectra of CDs (0.25 mg/mL)-OPD (100 μM) mixture in pH=6 MES buffer with 365 nm UV light irradiation; (b) light-driven oxidation induced color change of CDs-OPD mixture, top panel: under daylight, bottom panel: under 365 nm UV light; (c) time-dependent fluorescent emission spectra (Ex=400 nm) of CDs-OPD mixture in pH=6 buffer under UV light irradiation; (d) time-dependent light-driven oxidation of OPD with and without CDs. The fluorescence intensities were recorded at 558 nm under 400 nm excitation.

(a) Fluorescent emission spectra (Ex=400 nm) of CDs (0.25 mg/mL) and OPD (100 μM) mixture in pH=6 buffer with different amounts of Cu2+ ions under UV light irradiation for 4 min; (b) fluorescent emission spectra (Ex=400 nm) of CDs (0.25 mg/mL), OPD (100 μM), and Cu2+ ions (0.5 μM) mixture in pH=6 buffer with different amounts of Gly under UV light irradiation for 4 min; (c) schematic illustration of the detection mechanism for Gly.

(a) Digital photographs of photosensitization mixture with different amounts of Gly under 365 nm UV light; (b) the color analysis on a smartphone; (c) linear plots of (red channel)/(blue channel) versus the concentration of Gly.

(a) Ratiometric fluorescent responses to various pesticides. Detailed conditions: CDs (0.25 mg/mL), OPD (100 μM), Cu2+ ions (0.5 μM), pesticides (1 μM), 365 nm UV light irradiation (4 min); (b) the chemical structures of the corresponding pesticides.
Gly analysis in soil leachate by proposed method in this work.

head.ce:title: Luminescence and photosensitization performances of carbon dots for ratiometric fluorescent sensing of glyphosate

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Anhui Province Key Laboratory of Coal Clean Conversion and High Valued Utilization, Anhui University of Technology, Maanshan 243032, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering
Anhui Province Key Laboratory of Coal Clean Conversion and High Valued Utilization

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Anhui Province Key Laboratory of Coal Clean Conversion and High Valued Utilization, Anhui University of Technology, Maanshan 243032, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Exploring the practical applications of luminescent carbon dots (CDs) plays an important role in analytical chemistry. In this study, maleic anhydride and polyethyleneimine derived phosphorescent CDs can generate oxidative singlet oxygen and superoxide radical under UV light irradiation. The o-phenylenediamine (OPD) was used as a chromogenic agent, which can be oxidized by singlet oxygen and superoxide radical to produce intense yellow color fluorescence. Furthermore, the CDs-photosensitized oxidation of OPD was inhibited by Cu2+ ions. Because the high affinity between glyphosate and Cu2+, CDs-photosensitized oxidation can take place when glyphosate is added into the Cu2+-mediated system. Utilizing the blue color fluorescence of CDs as internal standard, a ratiometric fluorescent method for glyphosate detection was established. The proposed method can be performed on a smartphone, which exhibits a satisfactory performance for detection of glyphosate residue in real samples rapidly, conveniently, and effectively.

A ratiometric fluorescent method was established. The carbon dots serve as a photosensitizer to generate reactive oxygen species for chromogenic oxidation and a fluorescent emitter for internal standard.

ce:section.ce:para: Pesticides play a vital role in ensuring grain output growth and protecting world food security [1]. At the same time, the abuse of pesticides can lead to contamination of water and soil, endangering the ecological environment [2]. Glyphosate (Gly), a widely used and non-selective herbicide, has grown to dominate the weed control market since its commercialization in 1974 [3]. In recent years, the issue of biotoxicity of Gly has also attracted widespread attention [4]. Therefore, it is important to develop rapid and efficient methods for the detection of Gly. Many detection methods such as high performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) [5], mass spectrometry (MS) [6], surface-enhanced Raman spectroscopy (SERS) [7], electrochemical methods [8], and capillary electrophoresis [9] have been developed for the detection of Gly in environmental samples. However, these techniques still involve complex instruments and laborious procedures. For example, chemical derivatization of Gly is necessary for HPLC-MS analysis to improve the separation and detection performance [10].

Visual detection is a method that uses the naked eye or portable device to analyze the photochemical signal changes caused by some specific physical/chemical processes [11]. Luminescence-based visual detection can be used well in the quick identification of pesticide residues due to the simple operation and easy observation of results [12,13]. Among the developed approaches, various luminescent materials (such as organic dyes, quantum dots, and nanoclusters) were employed as response units [14–16]. Up to now, most of the reported luminescent visual detection is based on the activated singlet emissions of fluorescent materials.

Carbon dots (CDs), as a promising class of luminescent material, have been widely used in visual detection [17–18]. In recent years, fascinating researches have been carried out around the triplet state emissions of CDs [19–20]. The excited triplet state of CDs is activated by an effective intersystem crossing process (transition from the excited singlet state to the triplet state) under light excitation. The deactivation from the excited triplet state to the ground state generates long-lived phosphorescence emissions. On the other hand, reactive oxygen species (ROS) are generated from excited triplet state of CDs by the energy transfer or electron transfer in the presence of water and oxygen (O2) [21,22]. In this sense, CDs play the role of photosensitizers. However, the utilization of photosensitization properties of CDs to design pesticide sensors has not been investigated.

In this work, phosphorescent CDs with blue color fluorescence and excellent photosensitization performance were developed. The CDs dispersion can produce oxidative singlet oxygen (1O2) and superoxide radical (O2 •−) under UV light irradiation. The o-phenylenediamine (OPD) was used as a chromogenic agent, which can be oxidized by the 1O2 and O2 •− to produce yellow color fluorescence. The CDs-photosensitized oxidation of OPD is inhibited by Cu2+ ions. When Gly was added into the Cu2+-mediated system, oxidation can be carried out due to the high affinity of Gly and Cu2+, leading to the quantitative detection of Gly. By means of color analysis on a smartphone, a ratiometric sensor was constructed with the blue fluorescence of CDs as an internal standard, which provides a rapid way for Gly detection.

300 mg of maleic anhydride (MA) was added to 15 mL of polyethyleneimine (PEI) aqueous solution (10 mg/mL) until they were completely dissolved under ultrasonication. The mixture was transferred into a 25 mL of Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave followed by hydrothermal treatment at 220 °C for 8 h. After cooling down to room temperature naturally, the solution was dialyzed using the dialysis membranes (molecular weight of 500 Da) for 96 h. Finally, the CDs were obtained as brown powder by freeze-drying.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Oxidation of OPD using CDs as photosensitizer
ce:section.ce:para: Firstly, 100 μL of CDs dispersion (5 mg/mL) and 200 μL of OPD solution (1 mM) were mixed with 2-morpholinoethanesulfonic acid (MES) buffer (0.2 M, pH=6) to a final volume of 2 mL in a quartz cuvette. Then, the mixture was irradiated with a light emitting diode (LED, 365 nm) at room temperature. Finally, the absorption spectra, fluorescence spectra and color changes of the dispersion were recorded using spectrophotometer and smartphone.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of glyphosate by photosensitization-mediated oxidation
ce:section.ce:para: Typically, 100 μL of CDs dispersion (5 mg/mL), 200 μL of OPD solution (1 mM), 10 μL of Cu2+ ions (0.1 mM), and different amounts of glyphosate were mixed with MES buffer (0.2 M, pH=6) to a final volume of 2 mL in a quartz cuvette. Then, the mixture was irradiated with 365 nm LED at room temperature for 4 min. Finally, the absorption spectra, fluorescence spectra and color changes of the dispersion were recorded using spectrophotometer and smartphone.

Typically, 100 μL of CDs dispersion (5 mg/mL), 200 μL of OPD solution (1 mM), 10 μL of Cu2+ ions (0.1 mM), and different amounts of glyphosate were mixed with MES buffer (0.2 M, pH=6) to a final volume of 2 mL in a quartz cuvette. Then, the mixture was irradiated with 365 nm LED at room temperature for 4 min. Subsequently, the fluorescent emission images were taken using iPhone 12 under 365 nm UV light excitation. The RGB values of acquired images were analysed using the color recognition software on a smartphone.

The CDs were synthesized from maleic anhydride and polyethyleneimine under hydrothermal conditions (Fig. 1 a). Transmission electron microscope (TEM) characterization revealed that the CDs were monodisperse nanoparticles with size of ∼2.2 nm (Fig. 1b and c). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) indicated the presence of many kinds of chemical bonds in the CDs (Fig. S1), and Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectrum confirmed the chemical groups (such as –OH, –NH2, and C=O) on the surface of the CDs (Fig. S2).

The luminescent properties of CDs were investigated in detail. Since the absorption of CDs is mainly concentrated in the UV region (Fig. S3), the three-dimensional (3D) fluorescence spectra in the UV wavelength excitation range were tested (Fig. 1d). The results showed that the CDs mainly exhibited two fluorescent centers. The high-energy UV light (225–275 nm) excitation produced an emission peak at 430 nm, which did not move with the change of excitation wavelength. In contrast, the emission peaks under the excitation of long wavelength UV light (300–400 nm) exhibited obvious excitation-dependence (Fig. S4). The presence of two different luminescent centers may be related multiple decay channels [23]. The CDs had short-lived fluorescence emissions with fluorescence lifetimes of 34.6 and 10.2 ns under excitation at wavelengths of 250 and 330 nm, respectively (Fig. S5 and Table S1).

To confirm the generation of excited triplet states of CDs under light irradiation, the phosphorescent properties of CDs were investigated. However, the phosphorescence of CDs can not be observed in water dispersion due to the quenching of oxygen and collision with solvent (Fig. S6). Alternatively, the CDs were immobilized in polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) to make composite film. Long-lived triplet phosphorescence emissions of CDs were easily observed in composite film at room temperature. The 3D phosphorescence spectra showed that the CDs have a wide range of excitation and the emission has an excitation-independence characteristic (Fig. 1e and Fig. S7). The phosphorescence lifetimes of the CDs in PVA are 370 and 293 ms under excitation at 265 and 365 nm, respectively (Fig. S8 and Table S2). Benefiting from long-lived emissions, the phosphorescence of the CDs can be observed (∼2 s) after the UV excitation was turned off (Fig. S9).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Light-driven oxidation of OPD using CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The long-lived phosphorescence of CDs is derived from the radiative transitions from the excited triplet to the ground singlet state [24]. The developed CDs have outstanding phosphorescent emission, which indicates that the population of excited triplet states of CDs is easily under light radiation. Therefore, the CDs could be used as a photosensitizer in dispersion. To verify this hypothesis, OPD was selected as a substrate for light-driven oxidation using CDs as photosensitizer. In our experiments, the light-driven oxidation was performed using a commercial 365 nm UV light emitting diode (Fig. S10). As shown in Fig. 2 a, when the OPD solution was illuminated by UV light in the presence of CDs, a strong absorption peak at 421 nm was generated. This caused the colorless solution to turn yellow (top panel of Fig. 2b). UV light activates the excited triplet state of CDs, which in turn transfers the energy to dissolved oxygen or H2O molecules [25]. Accompanied by a series of photochemical processes, ROS was generated in this system. Afterwards, the OPD was oxidized by the ROS to yield oxOPD (2,3-diaminophenazine) with yellow color [26,27]. Similarly, the CDs can also induce the light-driven oxidation of 3,3’,5,5’-tetramethylbenzidine (TMB) to give out blue color (Fig. S11). The above results suggest that the CDs can indeed act as a photosensitizer to produce ROS.

Moreover, the oxOPD also exhibits excellent fluorescence properties (bottom panel of Fig. 2b). Under the 400 nm excitation, the oxidized mixture exhibited two emission bands (Fig. 2c). The fluorescence intensities of oxOPD at 558 nm enhanced gradually with the increasing of light exposure time. The inner filter effect was present in this system because of the spectral overlap between the fluorescent emission of CDs and the absorption of oxOPD. Thus, the emission of CDs at 460 nm was gradually decreased with increasing the oxOPD.

The most noteworthy point is that the OPD itself is also oxidized under UV illumination due to the autocatalytic oxidation [28]. We further performed the photosensitized oxidation kinetics experiments (Fig. 2d). The fluorescence intensity of oxOPD was quickly enhanced under UV light illumination in the presence of CDs. The intensity reached saturation at ∼8 min, because the OPD was completely oxidized to oxOPD. However, in the autocatalytic photo-oxidation reaction of OPD (under UV illumination without CDs), the generation of oxOPD occurred slowly before 4 min of light exposure. According to calculations using the saturated fluorescence intensity, only 9.5% of OPD was oxidized to oxOPD under 4 min of light exposure. In contrast, 40% of OPD was oxidized within 4 min of CDs-photosensitization. Therefore, the UV light irradiation of 4 min was chosen as the optimal condition for further experiments.

To identify the ROS produced by photosensitization of CDs, four ROS scavengers (NaN3, p-benzoquinone, catalase, and isopropanol for 1O2, O2 •−, H2O2, and •OH, respectively) were added to the light-driven oxidation system (Fig. S12). The results showed that the oxidation of OPD was effectively inhibited after the scavenging of 1O2 and O2 •−. Additionally, 1O2 probe (SOSG) and O2 •− probe (DHR123) were used to confirm the formation of these oxidative species (Figs. S13 and S14). Further characterization using electron paramagnetic resonance (EPR) spectroscopy revealed that light radiation of CDs in the presence of trapping agents gives rise to the corresponding characteristic peaks (Fig. S15). The above results indicated that 1O2 and O2 •− may be the main oxidative species generated during the oxidation process.

In order to employ the CDs-photosensitized oxidation of OPD for pesticide detection, metal ions were utilized to modulate the photosensitization process. The selection of metal ions was mainly based on two considerations: (i) specific metal ions are triplet state quenchers, which can effectively inhibit the photosensitization process; (ii) metal ions have strong coordination ability with pesticides, which can achieve the recognition of pesticides [29].

To this end, CDs-photosensitized oxidation of OPD with the addition of different metal ions was investigated. As shown in Fig. 3 a, the photosensitization process was effectively suppressed by Cu2+ ions, resulting in a significant decline of fluorescence intensity at 558 nm after 4 min of light exposure. Moreover, other metal ions have a slight influence on the photosensitization process (Fig. S16). The role of Cu2+ ion in weakening the photosensitization ability of CDs was investigated by kinetics experiment (Fig. S17). The generation of oxOPD occurred slowly in the presence of 0.5 μM of Cu2+. The oxidation of OPD decreased by 50% in the presence of Cu2+ after 4 min of exposure to light. The results confirmed the inhibition of Cu2+ for photosensitization ability of CDs. Due to the paramagnetic effect of Cu2+ ions, the triplet state energy of CDs is transferred to Cu2+ ions [30], which weakens the ability of photosensitization to produce ROS. Besides, The 1O2 and O2 •− are highly oxidative. The oxidation of OPD by photosensitization was performed within 4 min. Therefore, negligible oxOPD was generated by Cu2+ ions induced catalytic reaction [31].

Based on the reason that Gly contains amine, carboxylate, and phosphonate coordination groups [32], the influence of Gly on the CDs-OPD-Cu2+ photosensitization system was explored. As shown in Fig. 3b, a continual fluorescence enhancement at 558 nm was observed with the increasing of Gly concentration. The linear range can be fitted ranging from 0–1 μM and 2–10 μM, respectively (Fig. S18). The detection limit was estimated to be 0.06 μM by 3σ criterion. As expected, the high affinity between Gly and Cu2+ prevents Cu2+ from inhibiting CDs-photosensitization, restoring the production of ROS (Fig. 3c).

In the CDs-OPD photosensitization system, the oxidation product oxOPD emits yellow color fluorescence. The CDs present stable blue fluorescence emission under UV light excitation (Fig. S19). Meanwhile, Cu2+ and Gly have negligible influence on the fluorescence emission of CDs (Fig. S20). Therefore, a ratiometric fluorescence sensor for Gly can be built using the CDs as an internal reference. Here, the digital photographs of the resultant mixture after 4 min of light exposure were taken using a smartphone. As shown in Fig. 4 a, with the increasing of Gly concentrations, the output color of the mixture changes from blue to white and to faint yellow after light-driven oxidation. Combined with the color analysis application on the smartphone, the RGB values of each cuvette were obtained (Fig. 4b). The ratio of the red channel to the blue channel showed good linear relationships with the concentration of Gly (Fig. 4c). Therefore, the simple and fast analysis of Gly can be realized through UV lamp irradiation and digital image acquisition.

We also examined the selectivity of proposed method for the detection of Gly. As shown in Fig. 5 , when organophosphorus pesticides such as dimethoate, quinalphos, and malathion were added to the CDs-OPD-Cu2+ system, the photosensitization process inhibited by Cu2+ could not be recovered. In addition, other commonly used herbicides and fungicides such as atrazine, 2,4-D, and carbendazim have negligible response. Moreover, we tested biomolecules (including cysteine, Na2S, glutathione, proline, glutamate, and mannitol) and anions (including Cl−, SO4 2−, NO3 −, PO4 3−, and CO3 2−) in Fig. S21 and Fig. S22. These molecules and anions induced negligible recovery of the photosensitized oxidation of OPD. The above results show that our proposed method can selectively recognize Gly.

Finally, the possibility of this method for real sample detection was verified. Typically, CDs dispersion, Cu2+ ions, and OPD solution were mixed with the soil leachate, and the digital photographs were taken after UV light illumination. Through the evaluation of R/B values, the residual quantity of Gly can be achieved. The recoveries ranged from 95.70% to 107.96% when 0.2 and 0.5 μM of Gly were added (Table 1 ). The results confirm the proposed photosensitization method is a promising way for on-site detection.

In conclusion, maleic anhydride and polyethyleneimine derived phosphorescent CDs sensitized the generation of 1O2 and O2 •− under UV light irradiation. The photosensitization process was effectively suppressed by Cu2+ ions and restored in the presence of Gly. The OPD was oxidized by photo-generated 1O2 and O2 •− which resulted in the fluorescence of yellow color. Combined with the intrinsic blue fluorescence of CDs, the concentration of Gly can be measured by the fluorescence color analysis on a smartphone. Such interesting findings would promote the design of CDs-based chemical sensors and stimulate the application of visual detection.
Data will be made available on request.

Cheng ZHANG: Conceptualization, Investigation, Writing – original draft. Yu ZHENG: Investigation, Visualization, Writing – original draft. Xinyi LI: Methodology, Formal analysis. Lulu BA: Formal analysis, Validation. Caixia WEI: Visualization. Yawen GAO: Validation. Kui ZHANG: Supervision, Writing – original draft.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (22106005), the Foundation of Education Department of Anhui Province (2022AH030047), Anhui Province Key Laboratory of Coal Clean Conversion and High Valued Utilization (CHV21-05), and Innovative Training Program for College Students (202210360040).

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:10.1016/j.cjac.2023.100257.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Dual emission of singlet and triplet states boost the sensitivity of pressure-sensing
